{"step_by_step_thinking": "The query asks for the percentage of surgical site infection in patients with clean contaminated wounds after prophylactic antibiotic use. Document [1] states that for clean-contaminated procedures, the most commonly found organisms causing surgical site infections are skin flora, gram-negative rods, and Enterococci. Document [10] provides a definition of wound classes, stating that clean-contaminated wounds involve the respiratory, alimentary, genital, and urinary tract as long as the tract is entered without unusual contamination. Document [14] mentions that a systematic review and meta-analysis of SSI following MMS without prophylactic antibiotics found a mean infection range between 1.4% and 2.7%. Given that prophylactic antibiotics are typically used in clean-contaminated wounds to reduce the risk of infection, the percentage of surgical site infections would likely be lower than the 1.4% to 2.7% range without prophylaxis.
